snippets.title: PMF and PDF with SciPy
snippets.code:
from scipy import stats

# Bernoulli as Binomial(n=1)
print("P(X=1) Bernoulli(0.3):", stats.binom.pmf(1, n=1, p=0.3))

# Binomial: 7 successes in 20 trials, p=0.4
print("Binomial(20, 0.4) P(X=7):", stats.binom.pmf(7, n=20, p=0.4))

# Poisson: exactly 3 events when lambda=4.5
print("Poisson(4.5) P(X=3):", stats.poisson.pmf(3, mu=4.5))

# Gaussian PDF at x=0, mean=0, std=1
print("N(0,1) density at 0:", stats.norm.pdf(0, loc=0, scale=1))
snippets.explanation: Discrete distributions use `.pmf(k, ...)`; continuous Normal uses `.pdf(x, ...)`. SciPy names the Poisson rate `mu` (λ).

snippets.title: Categorical samples and softmax probabilities
snippets.code:
import numpy as np

logits = np.array([2.0, 1.0, 0.1])  # 3 classes
probs = np.exp(logits - logits.max())
probs /= probs.sum()
print("pi:", probs)

# one Categorical draw
k = np.random.choice(len(probs), p=probs)
print("sampled class:", k)
snippets.explanation: Softmax turns logits into class probabilities π_k that sum to 1; `choice` with `p=probs` simulates a Categorical trial.

snippets.title: Gaussian Naive Bayes (Normal class-conditional features)
snippets.code:
from sklearn.datasets import load_iris
from sklearn.naive_bayes import GaussianNB

X, y = load_iris(return_X_y=True)
clf = GaussianNB()
clf.fit(X, y)

# Each feature j in class k is modeled as Normal(mu_kj, sigma_kj^2)
print("class means shape:", clf.theta_.shape)
print("predicted class for first row:", clf.predict(X[:1]))
snippets.explanation: `GaussianNB` fits per-class per-feature Normal parameters and picks the class with highest posterior—linking Gaussian PDFs to classification.

snippets.title: Poisson regression sketch (log link)
snippets.code:
import numpy as np
from scipy.stats import poisson

# lambda(x) = exp(w0 + w1 * x) ensures lambda > 0
w0, w1 = 0.5, 0.2
x = np.array([0, 1, 2, 3])
lam = np.exp(w0 + w1 * x)
print("Poisson rates:", lam)

# log-likelihood for observed counts y
y = np.array([3, 5, 4, 9])
ll = np.sum(poisson.logpmf(y, mu=lam))
print("log-likelihood:", ll)
snippets.explanation: Poisson mean must be positive; the log link is standard. MLE adjusts w to maximize Poisson log-likelihood—parallel to GLMs for count data.
